hook: The most complete Andalusian medina in Morocco, raised by Granada's exiles
facts: Rebuilt from ruins after 1492 by Muslim and Jewish refugees fleeing the fall of Granada
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1997 for its Andalusian urban fabric
Nicknamed 'Granada's daughter'; some families still keep keys to their lost Andalusian homes
Among the smallest and least altered of Morocco's historic medinas
legend: They say certain old families still hold the iron keys to houses in Granada, passed down through five centuries in hope of return.
w: Raised from ruins after 1492 by Muslims and Jews fleeing Granada, who rebuilt their lost Andalusia in whitewashed lanes too narrow for two mules. UNESCO calls it the most intact Andalusian medina in Morocco. They say families still keep the keys to houses across the strait. Granada's daughter, in exile.

n: Royal Palace and Place Hassan II
hook: The old Feddan parade ground, now a marble square fronting the king's palace
facts: Place Hassan II, traditionally the Feddan, sits between the medina walls and the Spanish new town
The Royal Palace (Dar al-Makhzen) facade carries an ornate ceremonial gate
Long the city's main gathering ground, market and meeting point
Repaved in pale marble; the square links the medina to the Ensanche
w: Once the Feddan, the dusty parade ground where caravans unloaded and crowds gathered, now a sweep of pale marble. On one side, the king's palace and its carved gate; on the other, the medina wall. The hinge between Andalusian alleys and the Spanish grid. Where the city still comes to stand around.

hook: A wedding-and-weapons collection inside a sultan's 1830 bastion
facts: Housed in a fortress built around 1830 on the orders of Sultan Moulay Abd al-Rahman
Founded in 1928 as the Museum of Indigenous Arts; moved to Bab el Okla and reopened in 1948
Displays Tetouani bridal garments, ceremonial linens and everyday objects
Named after one of the medina's seven historic gates
w: A bastion thrown up around 1830 by Sultan Moulay Abd al-Rahman, now stuffed with the stuff of Tetouani weddings, bridal silk, embroidered linen, ceremonial weapons. A garrison turned into a record of domestic life. Defensive walls, full of dowries instead of guns. The gate of Bab el Okla, repurposed.

hook: Roman mosaics lifted from Lixus, in a garden of funerary stones
facts: Inaugurated on 19 July 1940 to hold finds from digs across northern Morocco
Garden displays five 2nd-century mosaics removed from the Roman city of Lixus
Holds material from the Punic-Mauretanian site of Tamuda, excavated from 1921
Also covers Kaf Taht L'ghar, M'zora and Ksar es-Seghir
w: Opened in 1940 to house what Spanish archaeologists pulled from the north, the garden alone repays the visit, five second-century mosaics lifted whole from the Roman city of Lixus, set among funerary stelae. Inside, the bones of Tamuda, dug from 1921. Provincial Rome, carried up the coast and laid out in a yard.

n: Dar Sanaa School of Traditional Arts
hook: An artisan school founded to keep dying medina crafts alive
facts: Founded in 1919 under the Spanish protectorate by painter Mariano Bertuchi and Abdessalam Bennouna
Stands opposite Bab el Okla, a gate of the old medina
Trains students in zellij, woodcarving, embroidery, leather and other endangered crafts
Workshops can be watched in progress by visitors
w: Set up in 1919 by a Spanish painter and a Tetouani nationalist to stop the old crafts dying out, students still bend over zellij, cedar and silk thread across from Bab el Okla. Half school, half rescue mission for skills the century kept trying to kill. Centuries-old techniques, taught to keep them breathing.

hook: Four tanners left where once there were over a hundred and twenty
facts: Set near the Royal Palace on the route toward the cemetery, inside the medina
Spread across roughly 2,700 square metres with some 380 stone wells
Only about four tanners still work the pits, down from more than 120
Skins are still treated in brick baths to make leather
w: Twenty-seven hundred square metres, three hundred and eighty stone wells, and four men left working them. Once over a hundred and twenty tanners cured skins in these brick baths near the palace; the trade has all but drained away. The smell hits first. A near-empty honeycomb of pits, slowly going quiet.

hook: The covered cloth market where Jebli women sell their striped mendils
facts: The medina's main covered textile marketplace
Rif 'Jebliate' women sell mendils, striped red, blue and white cloths
Rows of vendors trade traditional fabrics and clothing
The tanners' souq sits within the same market quarter
w: The medina's covered cloth hall, where Jebli women down from the Rif spread out their mendils, the striped red, blue and white wraps they weave and wear. Rows of fabric, the snap of bargaining, the tanners' pits leaking their reek next door. Commerce as it has run here for centuries, under one shadowed roof.

hook: A Sephardi quarter of Spanish balconies and sixteen lost synagogues
facts: Settled by Sephardi Jews who fled Spain after the Reconquista and Inquisition
Houses carry street-facing balconies, a Spanish style rare in Moroccan medinas
Sixteen synagogues were active here at the start of the 20th century
The Isaac Ben Walid synagogue honours the rabbi and author of Vayomer Yitzhak
w: Sephardim who fled Spain after 1492 built their quarter with balconies hanging over the street, a Castilian habit you won't see elsewhere in the medina. Sixteen synagogues once worked here; the Isaac Ben Walid still carries its rabbi's name. A second exile from Granada, written in iron railings and shuttered prayer halls.

hook: The Spanish new town's circular heart, ringed with rationalist facades
facts: Also called Plaza Primo, the centre of the Spanish-built Ensanche (1913-1956)
A neo-Mudejar Catholic church, Our Lady of Victory, was raised on the square in 1930
Surrounded by Art Deco and rationalist buildings, including the Equitativa block
Faced by the old Post Office and the historic Café París
w: Step through the medina wall and Morocco turns Iberian, a planned grid of Art Deco and rationalist blocks the Spanish laid out between 1913 and 1956. This roundabout square anchors it, fountain at the centre, a 1930 neo-Mudejar church on one flank, Café Paris on another. Colonial town-planning, frozen mid-flourish.

hook: A 1923 Spanish theatre that became a cinema, then dark, then back
facts: Designed in 1923 by architect José Gutiérrez Lescura
Reworked by Miguel de la Quadra-Salcedo between 1940 and 1942
Converted into a cinema around 1950
Closed in February 2015 and reopened by early 2022
w: Built in 1923 for Spanish audiences, reworked in the war years, then turned over to cinema around 1950. It went dark in 2015 and many assumed it had closed for good. Reopened by 2022, against the odds. A protectorate playhouse that has now outlived the empire that raised it. Curtain up again, somehow.

hook: The medina's great mosque, its minaret the highest point in the old town
facts: Built in 1808 (1222 AH) on the site of a 16th-century mosque
Stands near the old Jewish quarter's former location
Its minaret was raised as the tallest point in the medina, visible from far off
Decoration continues the long Andalusian architectural tradition; closed to non-Muslims
w: Raised in 1808 over a 16th-century predecessor, its minaret built to stand higher than anything else in the medina, a beacon you can pick out from the hills. The zellij and brickwork carry on the Andalusian line the refugees brought from Granada. Closed to non-Muslims, but its tower belongs to the whole skyline.

hook: The tomb of the Granadan captain who refounded Tetouan
facts: Holds Ali al-Mandari, the Granadan commander who rebuilt Tetouan in the late 15th century
He served Boabdil, last Nasrid king of Granada, before fleeing the Reconquista
Stands in a Muslim cemetery next to Bab Maqabar in the medina
Regarded as the city's founder and a venerated figure
legend: They say al-Mandari's widow, Sayyida al-Hurra, ruled Tetouan after him and turned corsair against the Christian fleets that had driven them from Spain.
w: Ali al-Mandari led troops for Boabdil, watched Granada fall, then crossed to a ruined town and rebuilt it into the city you now walk. His tomb sits in the cemetery by Bab Maqabar. Legend says his widow, Sayyida al-Hurra, took up where he stopped and turned pirate against the Christians who exiled them. The founder, come to rest.
